title: Malaise of Malware Attacks Wake-up call for cybersecurity in Bangladesh
short_description: Bangladesh had been on the battleground for nine bloody months during the 1971 liberation war against Pakistan to protect its sovereignty.
description_html:
Abstract

Bangladesh had been on the battleground for nine bloody months during the 1971 liberation war against Pakistan to protect its sovereignty. The same country has been on the front line in the 21st century, but this time it is not at war with any particular country like that real pitched battle. It has been on the battlefield with global cyber-criminals and their attacks umpteen times. We read reports of cyber-threats and attacks almost daily in our country and beyond its territory. Bangladesh often comes under virus warfare launched by hackers, hacktivists, info thieves and wire frauds in cyberspace or in the virtual world, making the real world unstable through breaking the ranks of security and the heist of important data, documents, information and money. Cyber-attacks have snatched away night’s sleep of IT advisers, managers and administrators of governments across borders. Bangladesh is no exception to this dire global phenomenon as hackers or unidentified people frequently haunt and hack websites through breaking into security firewalls. Cyber-threats have thus posed a big challenge to Bangladesh that has exposed its vulnerabilities to such attacks in recent years. Its banking system has always been at risk. Cellular companies and network operators seem doomed to suffer at the hands of cyber-criminals, attackers or hackers. Businesses are not immune too. The attacks have become a routine on the monthly phishing calendar of cyber-ogres. This paper tries to shed light on recurrent cyber-threats to, attacks on and challenges for Bangladesh. It suggests how this highly vulnerable country responds to such attacks and goes forward along with these challenges and uncertainties.

Keywords: Cybercrime, cyber-attack, cyberwar, Bangladesh, vulnerability, cybersecurity

Introduction

Bangladesh and the world entered the cyber age in the late 20th century and they now live with the ubiquitous culture of computers, information technology and virtual reality. We are also used to seeing the enslavement of man by technology. Technologies have made our life easier and smoother on one hand, but enslaved us completely on the other. Hardly a day goes by without our use of technologies–be it smartphone, computer or internet. But this very technology has two faces–virtue and vice. The good thing is the entire world has become a single global village. We are cosmopolitans now and communication is at our fingertips. But this very technology is damaging our relations, making us cry and ashamed, and rendering us losers. In this day and age, we are witnessing the perpetration of periodic cyber-attacks by hackers with intent to damage or destroy a computer network or system by using malicious software or malware. Are we aware of the negative side of technology and its far-reaching impact? We should. IT specialists, analysts and experts have argued that the threat to cybersecurity has been inflated across the world. Alarmists have already sounded premonitions that another Cold War has begun. The mightier are taxing the brains of the weaker. Many have gone a degree further and asserted that World War III is ongoing! This time the battlefield is cyberspace, the new domain of warfare. This article tries to exert the ins and outs of the issues for the avid readers of the Bangladesh Economia.

Cyber world vs real world

The world has become more cyber than real in the age of the super-speed fifth-generation digital and internet technology. We harbour twin identities now–citizens of a nation and netizens of a cyber world. As we become netizens, we get hooked to various internet-enabled gadgets and devices, the internet world and other virtual social or professional networks rather than real-world social gatherings and get-togethers. In the era of the rapid march of the cyber world characterised by technological advances and innovations, we are alarmed by phishing and spamming time and again. Our real world is tormented by cut-throat geopolitics in cyberspace where nations are engaged in the conflict of interest, with the barracudas out to grab the minnows. It is a high-stakes, dog-eat-dog world of cyber-power brokers. But what is happening in cyberspace, in Marshall McLuhan’s global village? Are we really happily living in the cyber age, in the age of information overload, in the age of social networks? The answer is not so far to seek.

The netizens are not staying unstressed in cyberspace. Smart devices have become an addition for us for their easy availability everywhere. The overuse of these gadgets is making us addicted. This has become more dangerous than drug addiction. The problem is acute among teenagers. Their academic results are in a downtrend for internet addiction. It is true that half of the telecoms customer spending goes wasted on Facebook, YouTube, TikTok, online games or pornography at night. It is because of the compulsive use of the internet rather than concentrating on their reading tables. Socialisation has hit rock-bottom over the years. The digital boom and bust, the free flow of information in this rat race world and the strong communication technology have made our life easier but tougher to survive amid cyber-uncertainties. Our relationship is becoming cyber and we are becoming motionless and emotionless with the bane of smartphones in the age of infobahn. It is as if science has given us speed but snatched away our emotion.

Hacking and cyber-attacks sans borders

Hacking and cyber-attacks are the daily dose of life in the digital world today. Just an earth-shattering piece of cyber-hacking news stunned all the Bangladeshis six years back on a balmy afternoon in February 2016. Have you forgotten the world’s largest reserve heist in Bangladesh Bank? That swindle shocked the emerging economy awhile in its onward march in progress. Anonymous, a hacktivist collective, broke into the bank’s SWIFT messaging network and plundered $81 million from its account with the New York Federal Reserve. This is only the tip of the iceberg of such financial fiddles globally. Then again a global pandemonium on 12 May 2017 is still a nightmare as the world awoke to a wave of cyber-attacks on tens of thousands of targets like businesses, corporate behemoths and state entities in 150 countries. The cadre of hackers forced British hospitals to turn away patients, affected Spanish firms like Telefonica and threw other state entities into chaos. We have since seen a spike in the attacks in cyberspace across physical territorial borders.

The world in full-scale cyberwar, then?

Ransomware was the key culprit behind the May 12 unprecedented global bedlam. That was not the beginning of the end. Cyberwar drags on as hacking has grown massively over the years, with new nations and companies getting targeted daily. Frantic acts hold businesses hostage for ransom through encrypting critical data or shutting down public or utility services. Hacking is no more a skilled coder’s hobby, for it is now a full-fledged profession! Hacking has grown into full-fledged cyberwar. Formidable as it is really! Cyberwar is the use of computer technology to disrupt activities of a state or an organisation, especially a deliberate attack on information systems for strategic or military purposes. Cyberwar benefits military goliaths much through exposing military might and weaknesses of a particular country. Since the beginning of the Russia-Ukraine war in February 2022, cyberwar and hacking between the two rivals have started, putting the world at stake. When real war using military hardware fails to bring any fruition, rival groups launch cyberwar against each other to incapacitate the enemy country’s data infrastructure. This is a serious crime against humanity. After World War I and II began the Cold War. Now comes the cyberwar. What’s next?

Internet world and cyber-terrorism

The entire internet world was in dread of a cyber-attack at the turn of 2022 as a vulnerability involving Java-dependent web server Apache Log4j was found on December 14. Cybersecurity experts spent days attempting to patch up things as hundreds of thousands of devices online were at risk of being attacked. This could allow attackers to craft malicious input data using a look-up pattern resulting in a denial-of-service attack. However, the cyber-threat research team of the Bangladesh Computer Council’s e-Government Computer Incident Response Team (BGD e-Gov CIRT) sent out a warning against the use of Log4j as it was marked as a critical vulnerability. But threats do not stop here. In its latest 2022 threat report, the state-backed agency has alerted Bangladesh against botnet and malware infections of and strikes on important data infrastructure, including in financial institutions, to forestall cyber-attacks on the sidelines of the Russia-Ukraine war.

Hundreds of major government organisations, including ministries and agencies, along with commercial banks in Bangladesh have already come under coordinated cyber-attacks in recent months. The CIRT reports that big organisations often undergo ‘distributed denial-of-service’ attacks. In their parlance, technologists say incoming traffic originates from many different sources during a DDoS attack, making it impossible to stop the attack by blocking a single source. DDoS is a type of cyber-attack from a wide botnet controlled by a cyber-criminal to target some specific IT infrastructure to disrupt regular service delivery. Such attacks can cost an organisation both time and money while its resources and services become inaccessible. In a sophisticated cyber-hack, 10-million customers of Australia’s number-two telecoms carrier Optus lost personal details in September 2022. Earlier, Intercontinental Hotel Group, the operator of hotel chains such as Crowne Plaza, Holiday Inn, Intercontinental and Kimpton, was targeted by an as-yet unknown threat actor in a cyber-attack. Cybersecurity leader Sophos warns cyber-terrorism in the coming days as cyber-attacks globally have risen in frequency and severity in recent years.

Why this havoc? How hackers make money?

The cyber world is devastated by the demerits of this otherwise digital boom today. A young internet demographic is involved in cybercrime globally. Instead of harnessing the potential of this digital dividend, errant techies use cyberspace as the most amenable platform for outrageous activities like hacking and crime. Millions of youths are being lured into the shadowy world of cyber-terrorism. Ransomware, the eponymous malware, blackmails corporate biggies through dysfunctioning their computer systems for money as it infects a machine and then encrypts or scrambles files so they can no longer be used or read. The files are only decrypted when a victim pays a ransom through cryptocurrency. As hacking grows, like-minded hackers join hands to work in alliance. Some arrest the world’s attention for good deeds like divulging any potential conspiracy or corruption. There are whistleblowers who leak classified documents of governments or high officials immersed in corruption. Such leaks are good for society. But most attract attention for their notoriety.

In the past couple of years, hackers have stolen sensitive and classified information from powerful countries and rammed home military strategies, judicial matters, major research findings, clinical trials, banking transactions and more. Ransomware is used with a worm, meaning that the infection of one computer can automatically affect an entire network. Ransomware is very infectious. Although ransomware is usually aimed at individuals, it now targets businesses for monetary gains. This virus quickly spreads to computers and then disables system applications for ransom. It locks up computers and holds a user’s files for ransom. The cyber-extortion worm damages, disables or even blocks access to computer systems until a sum of money is paid to the hacker(s). Media reports say the May 12 hack was perpetrated by an international cybercrime gang Black Shadow. That hacking is a serious crime that has attested to the loss of more than $6.0 trillion as damage so far.

How Bangladesh suffers in cyberspace

We know the entire world is currently awash with strings of cyber thefts, with Bangladesh being one of the worst victims of the digital Armageddon. Many such cybercrimes and scams, information stealing, credit card frauds are going unreported in any corner of the world almost daily counting heavy financial losses. In 2021, more than 150 public and private organisations came under attack, showing their utter vulnerabilities, according to the BGD e-Gov CIRT. Of the attacks, vulnerability accounts for 63.2%, intrusion or hackings 5.7%, malicious code 22.5% and abusive content 4.5%. The rest comprise fraudulence, intrusion attempts, service requests and information security. In an unstable global situation, Bangladesh is atop the list of countries at the highest risk of powerful Trojan attacks on state entities, banks and telecoms, according to a recent survey by anti-virus expert Kaspersky. It shows 3.69% of Kaspersky users in Bangladesh suffer Trojan attacks.

Cybercrime trends: Who are vulnerable?

Bangladesh has experienced a chain of back-to-back cyber-attacks by hackers and adversaries frequently in public and private organisations, be it government ministries, industries, businesses, banks, non-bank financial institutions, cardholders, insurance houses or telecoms carriers, and even individuals in recent times. The number of cybercrimes is also escalating as the country gained substantially in its ‘Digital Bangladesh’ journey in the era of digital transformation, fourth industrial revolution and artificial intelligence. But things still tell a different tale. This article endeavours to include some of the attacks that are a chilling reminder of how vulnerable we are to the merciless hackers and cyber-frauds. At the same time, such attacks forewarn us not to sit idle and safeguard national data resources and infrastructure from their filthy long arms.

Banks: A good number of public and private banks are currently in peril of unprecedented cyber-attacks, thanks to their indulgence and enforced idleness. Experts say a large percentage of digitally fragile banks are not taking precautionary measures to fend off the possible large-scale attacks and avert bigger financial loss. The malicious actors behind such transborder invasion include not only increasingly daring criminals like the Carbanak group, which targeted financial institutions to steal more than $1.0 billion during 2013-18 period, but also states and state-sponsored criminal gangland, according to a 2021 IMF report. In June 2022, a banking institute here surveyed the situation of the banking sector as of 2020 and found nearly 52 per cent of banks at grave risk of cyber-attacks.

Plastic cards: Bank cardholders are losing credentials to cyber-criminals amid outrageous attacks on the banking system, causing experts to cite the situation as too alarming and sensitive, as frauds have been making forays to steal card-users’ credentials and rob them. In its recent report, the BGD e-Gov CIRT said no debit/credit card is immune from cyber-attacks. Clients’ precious data is being stolen for infected bandwidth of mobile operators. According to an estimate, 300-400 cardholders lose financially to cyber-attackers per month for contaminated bandwidth–the blood of network communication.

Telcos: Telecoms carriers Grameenphone, Robi, Banglalink and Teletalk in Bangladesh come under coordinated cyber-attacks almost every month, indicating their diluted network communications and showing their utter vulnerabilities to such malware infections. The end users—the gullible subscribers—are the ultimate sufferers. Network operators have had a significant rate of infections of malware like ‘android.hummer’, ‘avalanche-andromeda’, ‘android.rootnik’ and ‘android.backdoor.prizmes’. State agencies concerned routinely call for telcos to maintain cybersecurity collectively through fortifying their network systems.

Cyber-trafficking: What worries Bangladesh and the world these days is cyber-trafficking. Trafficking in persons is being done in cyberspace aggressively. It so happens that human traffickers take to the digital boom of cyberspace and entice their gullible targets with so many promises before they are being smuggled abroad. Trafficking has witnessed a rise in popular social media networks like Facebook and TikTok in recent times.

Cyberbullying: The touch of communications technology has changed our life everywhere. Yet we see cyberbullying that takes place on social media, messaging platforms, gaming platforms and smartphones. Women are more victims of harassment and pornography in social media and men of mobile banking and ATM card hacking. Even more women are deceived during online shopping than men. Women and adolescents are increasingly falling prey to bullying, hacking, phishing, online violence and digital harassment. Their dignity, privacy and security are at stake. Trolling, abusing, threatening, stalking, voyeurism, body-shaming, defacing images, defaming, revenge porn and other forms of indecent representation of women are rampant in cyberspace.

Cybersecurity: What to do?

Bangladesh has achieved good progress in the era of digitisation. Due to a leap in the number of digital and IT-related crimes, the issue of cybersecurity has become a headache for government and private entities to stay protected against the criminal or unauthorised use of electronic data and make cyberspace safe. Cybersecurity is in an uproar as the volume, velocity and aggressiveness of cyber-attackers continue to grow. Bangladesh has been very vocal about digital security. It must include cyber-safety issues in academic curriculum and launch a mass campaign against cybercrime so that people get prepared to protect their identities in cyberspace and prevent such crimes.

Role of CIRT: The BGD e-Gov CIRT, which was born after the central bank’s reserve heist to fight any fatal intrusions further, has been vigilant in monitoring the country’s threat landscape and has played a critical role in sensitising stakeholders to the importance of cybersecurity. It routinely writes to all the key organisations, including important data infrastructure, requesting appropriate action to prevent such attacks in the coming days. It urges all to follow the ‘Digital Security Protection Guidelines for Critical Information Infrastructure’ and install/update anti-DDoS hardware and software, and set proper protection thresholds to keep all critical information infrastructure safe.

Be wary of phishing: Netizens must remain alert to stay safe online before falling prey to a phishing trap on the internet almost every day. They should be cautious about preserving any sensitive information and pictures regarding debit/credit card, ATM PIN, NID number, other passwords and important documents. The netizens should give a second thought as to whether it is safe to store them in browse-worthy, internet-enabled handsets or emails.

User awareness: Information technology and cybersecurity have become an inseparable bedfellow amid an alarming rise of cybercrime over the years. Experts are investing time and energy in ensuring security from crimes like cyberbullying, blackmailing, fraudulence or phishing or slurs for character assassination. Security measures include network security, app security, internet security, information security, operational security, cloud security, endpoint security or data security. Netizens should be smart in using the internet for their cyber safety and protection, and be wary if an email directs them to enter any kind of credentials.

Locking digital doors: As part of increased cybersecurity precautions, Joe Biden has wittily asked private companies and organisations to “lock their digital doors”, claiming that intelligence suggests its rivals are planning a cyber-attack on the United States. The UK cyber-authorities are also taking similar steps to stay safe. Experts suggest four essential steps or tips for netizens to follow in order to stay safe online. These are multi-factor authentication, strong password, regular software update and ways to identify phishing.

Cyber-insurance: Cyber-insurance can be introduced for virtual security as there is a direct relationship between cybersecurity and cyber-insurance in the wake of increased sophisticated cyber-attacks over the years globally. According to a Sophos report, 66% of global organisations surveyed were hit with ransomware in 2021. This challenge continues to grow. Optimising cybersecurity has become imperative for organisations.

Hotline against cyberbullies: Bangladesh Police have opened a dedicated hotline (+8801730336431) to help report online violence against, cyberbullying of and digital harassment of women. Victims can take action against obscene or abusive posts or pictures they receive on their social-media timeline, private message inbox or comments. They need to take screenshots of any indecent post or comment, gather links to the respective post and the profile of the abuser, and then report to their nearest police station.

Robot for women’s cyber-safety: Another major breakthrough is a special robot of the police cyber support for women that will ring an alarm in case of any emergency to protect female victims of cybercrime. An artificial intelligence (AI) tool or bot will take complaints from victims 24/7. Accordingly, the police will take protective action. Any victim can use this AI-controlled bot with a special code to lodge complaints.

What experts suggest?

Cybersecurity expert Tanvir Hassan Zoha suggests installing the ISO 27001 and the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard to thwart the rampant cyber-threats, and help organisations manage and keep their information assets safe and secure. Experts suggest developing a well-built monitoring system as most banks have yet to build it properly. They also propose the introduction of a central cybersecurity alert system to protect key-point installations from cyber-attacks.

About cardholders’ information piracy, they suggest that all the customers use verified and certified sites by different certified companies like Verisign while receiving their services that would help reduce such card fraud. Dr Md Mahbubul Alam Joarder, a professor at a technology institute, suggests stakeholders related to the banking ecosystem remain cautious to end such frequent card forgeries and protect the interests of their clients with the latest systems and solutions. The regulators concerned must play their appropriate roles in stemming such crimes. IT administrators of different public and private organisations can easily scuttle such attacks through writing six line commands in their systems.

Conclusion

As the use of internet technologies is increasing, so is cybercrime alarmingly. So, cybersecurity awareness is much more important today than any time before. It is time to curb cybercrime like phishing or ransomware attack, information piracy, cyberbullying, fraudulence, blackmailing and stealing of money online. It is high time to bring social networks under strict surveillance. The telecoms regulator BTRC’s cybersecurity cell must act rightly to protect privacy and ensure security of all netizens. Skilled manpower and professionals should be in place for stronger cybersecurity systems. Netizens should follow some netiquette while using the internet. They should dump those digital devices that pose security and privacy risks, and should not transact on public Wi-Fi or at cyber cafes and store any sensitive information on websites. IT professionals and cybersecurity analysts ask netizens to have strong passwords in order to protect their privacy and security as they come up with a rallying cry for caution to keep passwords of any type within the user. Each personal computer should have a strong firewall to block any unauthorised access while permitting outward communications. Based on intelligence reports, experts call on private companies and organisations to “lock their digital doors” as part of increased cybersecurity precautions. Bangladesh needs to explore the best possible ways to ensure digital security of the country through making people aware of its importance as the world is becoming more complex with the advent of newer technologies. The world is going through a very critical time. Well-thought-out plans can only tackle well-orchestrated cyber-threats and attacks. The faster the better for Bangladesh to stay cyber-smart.
